query: Vue v-if vs v-show interview question

competitors.label: Conditional Rendering
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue guide for v-if, v-show, lazy rendering, and toggle-cost trade-offs.
theirStrengths: The docs clearly explain lazy rendering, DOM presence, and the classic higher-toggle-cost versus higher-initial-cost trade-off.
theirGaps: They are lighter on component-instance state preservation, watcher work while hidden, and the cleanup-versus-hidden-work debugging angle.

realWorldUseCases: The real production/debug question is whether you want cheap toggles with preserved state or true teardown with zero hidden work.
followUpConfusion: Hidden-with- v-show components can still run watchers/computed updates; v-if removes them entirely.

realWorldUseCases: The official page already frames the decision around runtime toggle frequency and initial render cost.
actionableExamples: It gives direct v-if and v-show examples and explains DOM presence clearly, so example quality stays close.
followUpConfusion: The docs say v-if destroys and recreates listeners and child components, but they are less explicit about preserved local state, hidden reactive work, and cleanup behavior.

actionableExamples: Keep one ultra-short parent-child toggle example that shows state reset under v-if and preserved state under v-show.

competitors.winReason: The official docs cover the baseline trade-off well, but our page is more concrete on lifecycle, hidden reactive work, and preserved-state debugging.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because conditional rendering, lifecycle, and dynamic UI state are all part of its Vue fundamentals coverage.
theirStrengths: It is a broad interview-prep resource with worked Vue questions and lifecycle-related practice.
theirGaps: The landing page is not a dedicated v-if versus v-show explanation, so the concept-specific trade-offs are less explicit.

realWorldUseCases: Use v-show for frequent toggles; use v-if when the branch is rarely needed or expensive and you want zero cost while it’s off.
followUpConfusion: v-show does not support v-else / v-else-if chains; v-if does.

realWorldUseCases: The bank clearly covers dynamic Vue UI behavior and interview fundamentals, but it is less focused on this exact toggle-cost decision.
actionableExamples: Its solved-question model keeps the example bar reasonably high, so examples stay close.
followUpConfusion: The category page does not spell out the v-else support difference, hidden watcher work, or local-state reset behavior.

actionableExamples: Keep one tiny note that hidden v-show branches can still consume reactive work even when not visible.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is valuable as an interview hub, but our answer is much more explicit about preserved state, hidden work, and the practical toggle decision.

competitors.label: v-if vs. v-show - Vue Conditional Rendering
competitors.selectionReason: LearnVue has a topic-direct comparison article for the v-if versus v-show decision.
theirStrengths: It is a direct topic match and gives the core performance distinction in an accessible way.
theirGaps: It is lighter on component lifecycle consequences, hidden reactive work, and cleanup-driven debugging.

realWorldUseCases: With v-if , toggling false unmounts the child component (cleanup runs, subscriptions stop, DOM removed). Toggling true creates a fresh instance, so local state resets.
actionableExamples: With v-show , the child stays mounted the whole time; toggling only changes visibility. The instance, refs, and local state remain intact.

realWorldUseCases: The article explains the core rendering difference and performance trade-off clearly.
actionableExamples: It includes practical guidance on when each directive fits, but with less component-lifecycle depth than our example.
followUpConfusion: The follow-up discussion is less detailed on watchers continuing to run while hidden and on v-else support limitations.
Keep one small note on accessibility-tree removal under display:none so the follow-up remains rounded out.

competitors.winReason: LearnVue is a good direct comparator, but our page adds the stronger parent-child lifecycle example and turns the choice into a more practical debugging decision.
